Gwen haggardly swung open her front door, shoved the keys into her pocket,
flicked the lock in, and ambled through the red, sunset-lit corridor to sink
down on her couch–

‘Huh’, she paused, staring at the circle of rainbow and national flags that she
had hanging off a single pot on the coffee table.  ‘Something’s missing’, she
nearly mumbled, blinking.  The couch was right under her hand, but she hadn’t
sat down yet.

‘The red flag is missing’, she answered herself in the next moment.

Gwen approached the jar, peering all around it for any critter that could’ve
eaten or specifically hid the poor flag, but to no avail.  She narrowed her
eyes.  It was there for sure.  There was definitely a red flag there, because the
rest of the colors were also there.  ‘No.  I swear there were more flags than ten
in the jar, too,’ she peered closer, then closed her eyes.  ‘Right,’ she
summoned her imagination, ‘there were definitely a total of fourteen flags,
including the red one.  Then I must be missing two flags,’ she tapped the thin
leather couch while scrolling her memory.  ‘Has it always been this way? Because
I haven’t touched the flags since I got them,’ Gwen shivered, nervously running
her fingers together.  ‘I… I definitely didn’t.’

She threw a match into the fireplace, then stood still, staring at the feeble
sparks.  Should she call her friends? Or the police? Her dial phone–

When she looked away, her forehead throbbing, her eyes couldn’t help but be
drawn towards the cup of flags beside the dial telephone.  ‘What could it be?’
her nails drummed.  And in her stupor, she realized there were about a dozen
other things slightly amiss in the room.  Wasn’t the painting on the wall, the
one inscribed with scratchy eldritch letters, ‘F092dd… but it’s supposed to be
slightly off-centered.’ She had never bothered to fix it due to laziness, and
it gave her apartment a bit more charm, but she didn’t recall looking at it too
closely recently.  ‘Has it always been like this?’

But besides the painting, the newspaper pile was too neat: she’d usually only
hazardously toss over the material.  Additionally, the blurry, grayscale photo
of her high school graduation group had been slightly adjusted.  The towel rack
was actually neat, and, she had to walk over to check, the vase of fake roses
on the kitchen countertop had been replaced with fresh buds.  She touched the
rose petals with cool fingertips– no, it was definitely a living flower: she
could smell its perfume.  A shiver snaked down her spine and she stiffened,
blood rushing through her ears.

Her eyes seized upon an off-white crumpled surface, half buried into the soil.
A note! She reached in, but froze, ‘Is it even safe? …I guess I can just wash
my hands after,’ she sighed, and picked it up.  The paper was dry under her
touch, and she rubbed it slightly as she brought it to her eyes.

“I love you.”

The handwriting was a perfect cursive in clean, black ink.  What was most
frightening, though, was how familiar the handwriting seemed.

Gwen took a step back, reeling, and clutched the nearest object– the back of
the couch.  She whipped around, scanning the rest of her home, but obviously, no
one was there.  ‘But someone has been here,’ her hand grew white, her face
draining, lips paling, ‘That girl, she’s definitely been here.’ She clenched
her teeth.

‘Could she still be in here?’

Gwen looked up, rapping harshly on her leg.  What do I do?

She should call the police.  Or, she could investigate herself, or… she could
ignore it.  Act as if nothing’s happened.  She would… probably eventually go
away.

She needed space to think.

Moving towards the bathroom, Gwen suddenly saw all the other things in her home
that were slightly off-place.  Well, in-place, now, she supposed.  How did I not
notice this before? This clock, too… the living room clock.  It used to be four
minutes off the other one, she remembered the new analog clock in her bedroom
and all the times she had to calculate the offset when getting ready for work.

She almost felt like a stranger in her own home.

Gwen closed the bathroom door, quietly, and stared at herself in the mirror.
Her black lashes and curled hair gently curved down her shoulders, only
slightly ruffled from the day’s work, and her chapped lips were trembling.  Her
figure perfectly eclipsed the painting of a flower basket behind her so that
its dull background framed her instead.  She frowned.

She spun, taking the portrait and shifted it back to its slightly more crooked
position.  Only then did she relax; it’s getting too uncanny.

‘…Has it really bothered me that much?’ she shook her head to clear her mind.

Mutated just enough to make a difference in neatness– she didn't have OCD, but
with her, it damn seemed like it.  What was her problem? Gwen shut the bathroom
door– gently– and sunk down.  Pressed her palm against her heart.  Clenched her
eyes, pressed her mouth.  Released a breath.

All she could do now is to act normal.

Flags and clocks be damned, she won’t let her junior get to her.  Gwen didn’t
know exactly how she found out about where she lived, and it did slightly
trouble her, but she won’t let it get to her.  What's in the past is in the
past–

And nothing good would come out of poking the– well, it’s not even a bear.  She
was more like… a loving bear.  Gwen snorted.

She made up her mind.  ‘She didn’t need her in her life right now– she’s just
graduated college, she should be finding a job!’

Gwen twisted the bathroom doorknob, and entered her home anew.  She–

From the corner of her eye, she saw, right outside her window, a figure, pacing
the sidewalk.  She sighed, and slowly swirled around… 'can I do this?’ A
bittersweet smile melted into her dusky eyes, the remaining sunset casting a
deep maroon over her expression.  “Eiden,” she said, the name sliding softly
over her tongue.

By the time she got to her front entrance, the junior was already standing at
the open door, her tall silhouette carving into the magenta sky.  A baggy white
t-shirt and loose jeans draped over her body, her hair wolfishly unkempt as
always.

“Gwen–” the younger started, but she was already striding towards the
entrance.

“What- what the hell were you doing in my house?” she stormed.  “What are you
doing?”

“Gwen,” Eiden called out again, and the sound of her name spoken rippled
through the otherwise silent room, fluttering through the curtains and singing
over the rugs and pillows.

“No,” she stood firmly, or as firmly as she could, “I told you already–”

“It’s been six whole years,” Eiden uttered, daring to step closer.  Her gaze
drew up from the soil and into Gwen’s eyes, but there was no repentance.  “And I
still–”

“Where did you get my address?” she demanded, her heart racing.

“My friend told me you lived here,” and the woman still had the audacity to
blink innocently at her.  Gwen nearly groaned.  “I– trust me! Just trust me!”

“Get out,” Gwen said firmly, once and for all.  Eiden didn’t move.  “Get out!” Do
I always need to repeat myself? “I don’t…” her voice hesitated, and she
whispered, “You don’t–”

Eiden’s throat bobbed, swallowed, and she replied, “I’ll be different.  I’ll– I
won’t stalk you anymore,” she finally took a few steps backwards, raising her
hands.  Then she laughed, “No, I shouldn’t lie with you.  I’ll be with you, no
matter where you go,” her hands dropped as she said frankly, “Gwen, I will
always love you.”

The instant Eiden leaves, she’ll call the police.  No, will Eiden even leave, at
this rate? And even if she left, would she really be gone? Then, what were the
chances that if Gwen ran, that she’d get to the police station before Eiden
caught up to her? Eiden’s taller, but she isn’t physically trained like she is.

“No.” Gwen ducked under Eiden’s hand and escaped into the apartment hallway.
Fortunately, it’s a good fifteen minute sprint away, and Eiden didn’t have
access to a bicycle.

“Wait–!” Eiden gasped, instantly breaking out into a sprint to follow behind.

Gwen vaulted over rails, leapt down stairs in threes, and ran into the dying
remnants of the sun, chased by the night behind her.  She could hear the
footsteps rapidly drumming at her back and approaching her slowly, but surely.

The police station was only a mile away, really, ‘but this might become the
longest mile in my life,’ she thought as she sprinted through the street, over
the pavement, into the alley.  But Eiden was catching up, her legs were too
long!

No matter where Gwen went, Eiden was leaping over obstacles and full-sprinting
down stairs– ‘how am I supposed to compete with that?!’ she nearly cried, and
she wasn’t even halfway through her route when Eiden caught up to her and
pulled her into a hug.

She was sweating at this point, they were both sweating at this point, and the
hug was not helping.  But when Eiden released her, she was too exhausted to
continue.  Well, at least they were both tired.

Gwen found her back against the wall, and she leaned against it, resting her
head on the cold surface.  Aiden was panting, hands on her knees, and she nearly
laughed at the sight.  ‘Man, what am I even doing?’ a wild smile infected her
face, ‘Eiden, you always manage to catch up to me.’ Wait, no, now is not the
time to be reminiscing.  The smile dropped, ‘I’m supposed to be reporting this
bastard to the department–!’

Eiden approached her, and Gwen watched, keenly, unable to move, as she placed
her palm onto the wall, right besides her shoulder.

“What– what are you doing?” she hissed, and tried to push the arm away, but the
tender breath brushing over her face weakened her grip.  And she saw Eiden’s
warm eyes and the way her lips pursed, and she sagged.

“I’m trying to convince you to like me,” she leaned in.  “I–”

Gwen’s world widened.  She heard the buggies from the street.  The velvet stroke
of the wind.  The girl, now a woman, standing right in front of her–
